'''Sanuki no Kuni no Miyatsuko''' (''sanuki no kuni no miyatsuko'', ''sanuki kokuzō'') was a {{ill|kuni no miyatsuko|ja|国造}} who ruled the later {{ill|ritsuryō province|ja|令制国}} of {{ill|Sanuki Province|ja|讃岐国}}, present-day {{ill|Kagawa Prefecture|ja|香川県}}.

== Nihon Shoki ==
In the ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}'', {{ill|Emperor Richū|ja|履中天皇}}, Year 6, 2nd month, ''kichū'' new moon entry, {{ill|Washizumi-ō|ja|鷲住王}} — elder brother of {{ill|Futohime-no-Iratsume|ja|太姫郎姫}} and {{ill|Takatsuru-no-Iratsume|ja|高鶴郎姫}}, daughters of {{ill|Funamiso-wake-no-Ō|ja|鮒魚磯別王}} — is recorded as the ancestor of the two clans of the {{ill|Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|国造}}s of {{ill|Sanuki Province|ja|讃岐国}} and {{ill|Awa Province|ja|阿波国}}, {{ill|Ashikui-wake|ja|脚咋別}}. Washizumi-ō is written as having refused even repeated summons from {{ill|Emperor Richū|ja|履中天皇}}, suggesting he wielded formidable power in the provinces.

== Sendai Kuji Hongi ==
According to the ''{{ill|Sendai Kuji Hongi|ja|先代旧事本紀}}'''s "{{ill|Kokuzō Hongi|ja|国造本紀}}," during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Ōjin|ja|応神天皇}}, {{ill|Sumehore-no-Mikoto|ja|須賣保禮命}}, the third-generation descendant of {{ill|Kamikushi-ō|ja|神櫛皇子|lt=Kamikushi-ō|lt_ja=神櫛王}}, son of {{ill|Emperor Keikō|ja|景行天皇}}, was appointed Sanuki no Kuni no Miyatsuko.

== Shoku Nihongi ==
In the ''{{ill|Shoku Nihon Kōki|ja|続日本後紀}}'', {{ill|Enryaku|ja|延暦}} 10, 9th month, 18th day entry, an application for a surname change by {{ill|Ōshi-no-Atai Chitsugi|ja|凡直千継}} is recorded. According to it, the ancestor of the Ōshi-no-Atai clan was called the Hoshi-no-Atai clan, and during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Bidatsu|ja|敏達天皇}} they were granted the surname "Sanuki Ōoshi-no-Atai" as Sanuki no Kuni no Miyatsuko; in the {{ill|Kōgo-nen-jaku|ja|庚午年籍}} register, "Ōoshi" was changed to "Ōshi-no-Atai," and in the {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代}} they became "Sanuki-no-Atai" or "Ōshi-no-Atai." The applicant Chitsugi and others were permitted to change their surname to "{{ill|Sanuki clan|ja|讃岐氏|lt=Sanuki-no-Kimi|lt_ja=讃岐公}}" on account of their ancestors' achievements.

== Kogo Shūi ==
The ''{{ill|Kogo Shūi|ja|古語拾遺}}'' section on the Three Deities of the Heavenly Center and their clan ancestry records that {{ill|Taoki-ho-oi-no-Mikoto|ja|手置帆負命}} was the ancestor of the {{ill|Sanuki Imbe clan|ja|讃岐忌部氏|lt=Sanuki Imbe|lt_ja=讃岐忌部}}. The section on the Sun Goddess's retreat into the rock-cave records that when {{ill|Amaterasu|ja|天照大神}} hid in the {{ill|Ama-no-Iwato|ja|天岩戸|lt=Heavenly Rock-Cave|lt_ja=天の岩戸}}, {{ill|Taoki-ho-oi-no-Mikoto|ja|手置帆負命}}, together with {{ill|Hikosashiri-no-Mikoto|ja|彦狭知命}}, used the {{ill|Ama-no-Mihakari|ja|天津御量|lt=Ama-no-Mihakari|lt_ja=天御量}} (''amatsu-mihakari'') to fell the trees of great and small valleys and constructed the {{ill|Mizu-no-Miaraka|ja|瑞御殿|lt=Mizu-no-Miaraka|lt_ja=瑞殿}} (''mizu-no-araka''), as well as the august hat, halberd, and shield. It is written that the descendants of these two deities lived in {{ill|Nakusa District|ja|名草郡}} of {{ill|Kii Province|ja|紀伊国}}, and that the Imbe who felled the trees were called Miki (''miki''), and the Imbe who constructed the palace were called Araka (''araka''). In the section on the Imbe as makers of ritual implements, it is written that a branch of the {{ill|Kii Imbe|ja|紀伊忌部}} — who resided in {{ill|Kii Province|ja|紀伊国}} as woodcutters and palace builders — settled in {{ill|Sanuki Province|ja|讃岐国}}, a producer of wood, in search of materials for making halberd shafts; this was the beginning of the {{ill|Sanuki Imbe clan|ja|讃岐忌部氏|lt=Sanuki Imbe|lt_ja=讃岐忌部}}<ref>{{Cite book|和書 |title=古語拾遺／斎部 広成, 西宮 一民｜岩波文庫 - 岩波書店 |url=https://www.iwanami.co.jp/book/b245969.html |language=ja |date=1985/03/18 |publisher=岩波書店 |isbn=9784003003510 |author=斎部　広成}}</ref>.

== Harima no Kuni Fudoki ==
In the Ayabe-no-Sato entry of the {{ill|Shikama District|ja|飾磨郡}} section of the ''{{ill|Harima no Kuni Fudoki|ja|播磨国風土記}}'', it is recorded: "It is called Ayabe because {{ill|Han people|ja|漢人}} from {{ill|Sanuki Province|ja|讃岐国}} came here and settled. Hence it was named Ayabe." This suggests that immigrant clans from {{ill|Sanuki Province|ja|讃岐国}} were deeply involved in the cultivation of {{ill|Shikama District|ja|飾磨郡}} in {{ill|Harima Province|ja|播磨国}}<ref>{{Cite web |title=播磨風土記 {{!}} NDLサーチ {{!}} 国立国会図書館 |url=https://ndlsearch.ndl.go.jp/books/R000000050-I000039969 |website=国立国会図書館サーチ（NDLサーチ） |access-date=2025-04-11 |language=ja |publisher=国立国会図書館}}</ref>.

Additionally, the section on {{ill|Innami District|ja|印南郡}} records the tale of {{ill|Empress Jingū|ja|神功皇后}} seeking stone from {{ill|Hawaka|ja|羽床村|lt=Hawaka|lt_ja=羽若}} in {{ill|Sanuki Province|ja|讃岐国}} for the construction of a {{ill|kofun|ja|古墳}} for the deceased {{ill|Emperor Chūai|ja|仲哀天皇}}. The area around Mt. Washi is a {{ill|tuff|ja|凝灰岩|lt=tuffaceous|lt_ja=凝灰岩質}} zone and a site where easily-workable stone was quarried; hollowed-out {{ill|sarcophagi|ja|石棺|lt=hollowed-out sarcophagi|lt_ja=刳抜式石棺}} in various stages of manufacture have been found nearby, and hollowed-out sarcophagi of Mt. Washi stone have been excavated not only within {{ill|Kagawa Prefecture|ja|香川県}} (including at the {{ill|Iwaseo-yama Kofun Cluster|ja|石清尾山古墳群}}) but also in {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|ja|大阪府}}<ref>{{Cite web |title=石船石棺｜高松市 |url=https://www.city.takamatsu.kagawa.jp/kurashi/kosodate/bunka/bunkazai/shiteibunkazai/koko/ishifune.html |website=www.city.takamatsu.kagawa.jp |access-date=2025-04-11 |publisher=高松市埋蔵文化財センター}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |title=讃岐国分寺の考古学的研究 |url=https://ndlsearch.ndl.go.jp/books/R100000002-I024244410 |website=国立国会図書館サーチ（NDLサーチ） |access-date=2025-04-11 |language=ja |publisher=国立国会図書館 |author=渡部明夫}}</ref>.

== Ruijū Kokushi ==
According to the ''{{ill|Ruijū Kokushi|ja|類聚国史}}'', {{ill|Tenchō|ja|天長}} 4 ([[827]]), 1st month, ''kasshin'' 22nd day entry, the ancestor of {{ill|Saeki-no-Atai Toyoo|ja|佐伯直豊雄}}, {{ill|Ōtomo-no-Takebi-no-Muraji|ja|大伴武日|lt=Ōtomo-no-Takebi-no-Muraji|lt_ja=大伴健日連}}, accompanied {{ill|Yamato Takeru|ja|ヤマトタケル|lt=Yamato Takeru-no-Mikoto|lt_ja=倭武命}} during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Keikō|ja|景行天皇}} in pacifying the {{ill|Eastern Provinces|ja|東国}}, and for this merit was granted {{ill|Sanuki Province|ja|讃岐国}}. {{ill|Yamatoko-no-Muraji-no-Kimi|ja|倭胡連公}}, son of {{ill|Ōtomo-no-Takebi-no-Muraji|ja|大伴武日|lt=Ōtomo-no-Takebi-no-Muraji-no-Kimi|lt_ja=大伴健日連公}}, son of Takemochi Ōmuraji-no-Kimi, first son of Muroya Ōmuraji-no-Kimi, descendant of Mimono {{ill|Sukune|ja|宿禰|lt=Sukunei|lt_ja=宿爾}}, was appointed Sanuki no Kuni no Miyatsuko during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Ingyō|ja|允恭天皇}}.

== References ==
* Katō Kenkichi et al. (eds.), ''Nihon Kodai-shi Chimei Jiten'', Yūzankaku, 2007, ISBN 978-4-639-01995-4, p. 748

== Footnotes ==
{{脚注ヘルプ}}
{{Reflist}}

{{Japanese-history-stub}}
{{DEFAULTSORT:さぬきのくにのみやつこ}}
[[Category:讃岐国]]
[[Category:香川県の歴史]]
[[Category:Kuni no miyatsuko]]
[[ja:讃岐国造]]
[[Category:Need translation]]
